# Questions in Livestream 174

Live Stream #174: The Shroud With The X-Ray Eyes

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:55</a> | Have any biblical manuscripts been found in the older paleo-Hebrew script? | Yes. He mentions small early pieces and some Dead Sea Scroll usage in paleo-Hebrew. | Yes. He says some manuscripts and small pieces are written in paleo-Hebrew, including the Ketef Hinnom phylacteries and some Dead Sea Scrolls. He notes that the Qumran paleo-Hebrew manuscripts are not necessarily very old, dating around the late third to second centuries BC, but they show the older script lingering after the Aramaic script had become dominant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=673" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:13</a> | When did solar adoration become a thing in Egypt? | He says it appears at least by the early dynastic period, with major cult development by Dynasty 3. | He places solar worship at least in Dynasties 1 and 2, when Egyptians were already adoring the sun god Re. By Dynasty 3, he says Heliopolis had become a major cult center with temples and cults for Re, though he is not sure solar worship is earlier than all other Egyptian worship forms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=771" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:51</a> | Is Don Redford's Hyksos-memory explanation for the Exodus a taught Egyptological method? | No. He treats it as Redford's own minority reconstruction rather than standard training. | No. He says Egyptologists are not taught a method of recasting historical memories in that way; Redford developed the idea himself. He identifies it with a broader memorial view that links Hyksos expulsion traditions with Israelite Exodus memory, but he treats Redford's biblical work as weak even while respecting his Egyptological work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=1225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:25</a> | What qualifications did Paul or James have to be apostles besides witnessing the resurrection of Christ? | He says seeing the risen Christ was not enough by itself; apostleship required appointment or selection by Christ. | Seeing the risen Christ was one qualification, but not the decisive one by itself, because many people witnessed the resurrection without becoming apostles. He describes apostleship as an appointed office or inner-circle role: Christ had to select the person, which is why he treats Paul and James as apostles because they were chosen, not merely because they saw the risen Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=1747" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:07</a> | Besides taking numbers literally, what fundamentalist assumptions still cause trouble? | He highlights a wooden literalism that flattens genre, imagery, and idiom. | He points to a broader hermeneutic of taking absolutely everything literally, not only numbers. His example is Revelation's sword from Christ's mouth: some readers insist it must be a physical metal sword because the text says it, rather than recognizing imagery, genre, idiom, and the one-interpretation fallacy that often accompanies that wooden approach. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=2467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:07</a> | Do you plan to respond to Capturing Christianity's rebuttal of your Shroud video? | Yes. He says a response is coming and stresses that his aim is not to destroy faith but to avoid weak arguments. | Yes. He says their response to Capturing Christianity's Shroud rebuttal was scheduled to come out that Monday. He stresses that he is not trying to destroy anyone's faith in the Shroud of Turin, but to keep Catholics and others from relying on a poorly supported study; in his view, faith should be strengthened by solid arguments rather than vulnerable ones. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=2975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:35</a> | When Genesis says Adam was "with" Eve, does that mean he was right beside her or only that they were both in the garden? | He says Adam seems to be nearby or waiting in the wings, though the wording leaves some interpretive difficulty. | He reads Genesis 3:6 as suggesting Adam is present or at least nearby, almost waiting in the wings while Eve eats and then gives the fruit to him. He admits the exact location is uncertain, but he finds the scene strange because Adam appears to be watching rather than intervening. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=3359" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:59</a> | Is there a way to show that egalitarianism is not sinful without arguing that complementarianism has no biblical warrant? | He says the question should be reversed: the burden is to show that egalitarianism is sinful. | He says the question puts the burden in the wrong place because one cannot really prove a negative. In his framing, the real question is whether egalitarianism can be shown to be sinful; even if someone argues complementarianism is ideal or has warrant, that does not by itself prove egalitarianism is sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=3522" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:42</a> | Did you read about the recently discovered First Temple-period seal amulet from Jerusalem with an Assyrian winged figure? | Yes. He says they plan to cover it, calls it a beautiful seal, and thinks the public description is close but not quite right. | Yes. He says they had read about it and planned a "Mana Machine Alert" on it soon. He describes the object as a very interesting and beautifully made seal, but says the public description is not quite right, though close enough that he wants to save the fuller explanation for the coming video. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=3592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:52</a> | What is the difference between a genealogy and a king list? | He says a genealogy can serve many purposes, while a king list mainly tracks succession of leadership. | He compares a genealogy to a Swiss army knife because it can honor people, teach lessons, reveal inconvenient truths, be spliced or idealized, and carry literary purposes beyond lineage. A king list is more narrowly focused on succession: it tells who ruled a region or country, while a genealogy can communicate much more than who came next. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=3894" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:54</a> | What does it mean for the ground to be cursed in Genesis 3:17? | He says it means the land becomes unproductive: people labor and plant, but the ground does not yield as it should. | He explains the curse as unproductive land: people plant, till, and labor, but the ground resists them and may yield little or nothing. The point is not that agriculture becomes impossible, but that food now comes through struggle because the ground no longer simply responds fruitfully to human work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=4024" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:04</a> | Does Genesis 3:18 mean there were no thorns and thistles before the fall? | No. He reads the point as cursed agricultural labor, not necessarily the first existence of those plants. | No. He says Genesis 3:18 is about the agricultural situation after the fall: Adam leaves the garden setting and must farm cursed land that produces unwanted plants instead of crops. Thorns and thistles are the kinds of pests that grow in poor conditions, not necessarily brand-new plants that did not exist before. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=4335" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:15</a> | Is there monotheism in Tolkien's Middle-earth? | Yes. He describes Tolkien's world as a kind of monarchical monotheism centered on Eru Iluvatar. | Yes. He describes Tolkien's world as monotheistic, with Eru Iluvatar as the creator God at the top of the hierarchy. Beneath him are lesser divine or angelic beings, so Falk calls it monarchical or monolithic monotheism rather than a trinitarian model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=4683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:03</a> | Where can I read about the priestly ties of baptism? | He points toward Second Temple literature, but says it is not his area of expertise and does not give a firm recommendation. | He says the topic belongs in Second Temple literature and admits it is not his area. He vaguely remembers an older anti-Christian apologetics book with a useful section on baptism's origins and priestly ties, but he does not recommend the book and cannot give a current title, only suggesting that better works likely exist now. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=4815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:15</a> | What does the Spirit do in communion? | He says the Spirit creates communion and unity as the gathered church remembers and honors Christ. | He connects the answer to the word communion itself: in the Lord's Supper the church communes with God and the Holy Spirit. In his view, the Spirit creates unity in the local church as the gathered people do the same act together in honor and remembrance of Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=5078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:38</a> | What do you mean when Paul forbids being a drunkard but not simply being drunk? | He treats "drunkard" as habitual, uncontrolled drunkenness rather than every instance of intoxication. | He distinguishes occasional intoxication from being a drunkard, which he defines as habitual, uncontrolled, dysfunctional drunkenness that damages work, relationships, family, and one's walk with God. He notes that some Israelite feasts allowed excess drinking, but making drunkenness a lifestyle is the problem Paul addresses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=5435" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:35</a> | Were ancient Egyptians hard drinkers? | Sometimes, especially where wine was available, though he says ordinary beer was usually too weak to be the main problem. | He says some Egyptians were hard drinkers, but it depended on status and access. Poorer people generally could not afford wine, and ordinary beer was often only about one to three percent alcohol and served as safer, nutritious hydration; serious intoxication came from wine, and especially wine combined with lotus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=5706" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:06</a> | Were slaves in the ancient Near East seen as property? | Yes, though he says ancient slavery could still include side earnings, property ownership, and complicated social arrangements. | Yes, slaves could be bought and sold, but he says ancient Near Eastern ownership was not always total in the way modern readers might assume. Slaves could sometimes earn money, own property, own other slaves, or be released after a limited term, so he compares some arrangements more to a lease than ownership of every aspect of the person. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=6327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:27</a> | Is "be blameless" in Genesis 17:1 a command or a condition of walking before God? | He reads it less as a separate command and more as a statement about being complete or whole before God. | He reads "be blameless" through the idea of being complete or whole, and says it does not seem to function as a separate condition for walking before God. Instead, walking before God and being complete or blameless go together: one cannot really be blameless apart from walking before God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=6486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:06</a> | How does Isaiah 6:6-7 work if the Hebrew says the sin is covered rather than forgiven? | He says the Hebrew is about covering, which points to ritual atonement imagery rather than a simple modern gloss of forgiveness. | He argues that the Hebrew verb is about covering, not simply forgiving. Using white-out as an analogy, he says Old Covenant sacrifice covers sin so God's redemptive plan can continue, but it does not remove sin entirely; only Christ's blood ultimately takes sins away, so he thinks "forgiven" is a misleading translation here. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=6833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:53</a> | What is wrong with Dan McClellan? | He criticizes McClellan as progressive, prone to quote-mining and poisoning the well, and not a reliable teacher. | He gives a strongly negative assessment, calling McClellan a very progressive scholar and unreliable teacher. His main criticisms are that McClellan quote-mines, misrepresents opponents, poisons the well, uses inflammatory language and fallacies, and dismisses God-centered readings as mere dogma rather than scholarship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=6995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:35</a> | Is there an issue with sola scriptura? | Not if it is understood correctly as Scripture as final authority rather than Scripture as the only source of doctrine. | He says most objections attack a caricature. In his distinction, sola scriptura means Scripture is the final or ultimate authority, not that Christians consult no tradition, teachers, or church fathers; that stricter "Bible only" view is what he calls nuda scriptura. A real debate would have to address whether tradition is subordinate to Scripture or coequal with it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=7446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:06</a> | How should we understand the law as mostly case law, as tutor, and as unable to make anyone righteous? | He says the law reveals and teaches sin; it is not a mechanism by which people are justified. | He says case law still identifies real offenses; the case-law form mainly helps determine punishment and judicial discretion. The law teaches by showing that a transgression has occurred and by exposing sin, but even when mercy or restitution affects the penalty, the offender remains unrighteous under the law and cannot be justified by works of the law. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=7934" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:14</a> | How did ancient Egypt keep track of land claims when floods wiped out boundary markers? | He says officials resurveyed after the inundation and reallotted the same quantity of land rather than the exact same plot. | He says the Nile flood wiped out many boundary markers, so officials surveyed again after the inundation. Families received the same amount of land year after year, recorded publicly, but not necessarily the exact same physical acre because the flood had changed the landscape. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=8046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:06</a> | How do the horses in Zechariah differ from those in Revelation? | He says they seem to serve the same divine-vengeance role, but Revelation gives more detail about what the horsemen do. | He says Revelation is drawing on Zechariah, so the horses have the same basic job and belong to the Lord's vengeance. Revelation states more specifically what they do, such as war, famine, and death, while Zechariah is less explicit; he also notes Zechariah's imagery is chariot-based, whereas later readers often picture bareback riders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=8497" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:37</a> | If a pastor asks me to lead an adult Sunday school class and the elders are unwilling, what should I do? | He says someone still needs to teach; if the elders and pastor will not do it, the caller may have been given the task. | He says the elders should teach and that it is part of their responsibility, but if they declined and the pastor has asked this person to teach, then someone still has to do it. Since the concern was already raised, he tells the caller to accept the task as a calling and use it as an opportunity to learn and serve. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=9410" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:50</a> | When does John 1:19-2:12 take place in relation to Jesus' baptism and wilderness temptation? | He places the material after Jesus' baptism, while noting the sequencing across the Gospel accounts is not simple. | He thinks John 1 takes place after Jesus' baptism, partly because John the Baptist speaks as one who has already seen the Spirit descend on Jesus. He notes that harmonizing this with the wilderness temptation is not simple, and he does not force a fully tidy sequence across the Gospel accounts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=10942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:22</a> | Do you know a good agent for science-fiction writing? | He does not have one to recommend yet, and says agent selection is personal and tied to representation, coaching, and negotiation. | He says he has not yet found the right agent for himself and therefore cannot recommend one. He treats agent selection as very personal because the agent represents the author, helps negotiate terms, and may also coach and advise the writer through the publishing process. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=11108" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:08</a> | Does Paul imply pre-Adamic people in Romans 5:12-14? | He does not take the passage that way; he reads Paul as discussing sin and death from Adam to Moses. | He does not see pre-Adamic people in the passage. His reading is that Paul is tracking sin and death from Adam through Moses, emphasizing that sin was in the world even before the law, rather than making a claim about humans before Adam. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=11312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:32</a> | In Job 35:6, is Elihu saying our sin does not affect God even though sin offends a holy God? | He warns that Elihu is not automatically right, since Job's speakers can say things Scripture reports without endorsing. | He cautions that Elihu's statements should not automatically be treated as correct simply because they are in Job. The book reports speeches by multiple characters, and those speeches can include mistaken claims; therefore the verse needs to be handled as Elihu's argument, not necessarily as the book's final theological endorsement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=11552" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:32</a> | Is taking offense a type of being hurt? | Not necessarily. He distinguishes personal hurt from an affront to justice or holiness. | He distinguishes being personally hurt from taking offense at something that violates justice, holiness, or proper order. In that sense, offense can be about a moral affront rather than wounded feelings, so the categories overlap only sometimes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=11782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:22</a> | Is it wise to use wisdom from Buddhism, Taoism, or Confucianism to articulate Christianity? | He says missionaries have long debated this and that both cautious and permissive answers have real concerns. | He says Christians and missionaries have debated this kind of contextualization for the whole history of the church. Some argue it helps people understand Christianity, while others worry it communicates that the previous religion was already right; he gives no firm answer, but says anyone doing it must be very careful because both concerns have merit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=11983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:43</a> | What did you think of the discussion between Michael Jones and Dan McClellan? | He says McClellan poisoned the well repeatedly and that Jones handled the exchange well. | He says McClellan repeatedly poisoned the well by attributing Jones's conclusions to dogma rather than addressing the arguments. Falk thinks Jones responded well, especially by forcing attention back to evidence and even pointing to page numbers when McClellan denied a quoted source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=12170" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:50</a> | Have you seen the Doctor Who "Pyramids of Mars" story arc, and how accurate is its Egyptian lore? | Yes. He says it inspired his interest in Egyptology, though the Egyptian lore is highly stylized. | Yes. He says that Doctor Who story inspired him as a child to pursue Egyptology and that he still loves the episode. He does not consider the Egyptian lore strong or realistic, calling it highly stylized, but he says the story is imaginative and enjoyable rather than a serious Egyptological presentation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=12599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:59</a> | Can Bible believers accept the documentary hypothesis? | Yes, in some forms. He rejects it because he thinks it is wrong, not because every version is anti-biblical. | Yes, he says a Bible-believing Christian could accept the documentary hypothesis, though not necessarily every version of it. He personally rejects it because he thinks it is incorrect and leads to inferior readings of Scripture, not because holding any version of it automatically makes someone anti-biblical or unsaved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=12737" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:17</a> | What is the worst reading that comes out of the documentary hypothesis? | He points to the Ishmaelite/Midianite reading in the Joseph story as an especially poor example. | He identifies the Ishmaelite/Midianite treatment in the Joseph story as one of the worst examples. In his view, documentary-hypothesis readers wrongly split the text into conflicting sources, while the terms can work together because one can be an ethnic or tribal designation and the other a geographic designation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=13284" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:24</a> | Do you know anything about the remains of the city of Aten near Luxor? | He says it is a large site still under excavation and potentially important for the Amarna-period administrative picture. | He says the city of Aten lies between Medinet Habu and Deir el-Medina and is a large site still being excavated. He describes it as an Amarna-period administrative capital where Akhenaten tried to relocate bureaucracy from Memphis, and he notes that many buildings and potsherds have been found but, surprisingly, not texts yet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=13596" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:36</a> | In John 14:12-14, is Jesus saying he will give us anything we ask, or is the message for the apostles? | He says the promise is tied to acts of ministry and faith, not to asking for anything whatsoever. | He says the promise is not a blank check for any desire. It is tied to acts of ministry, acts of faith, doing the Father's will, and asking in Christ's name so that the Father is glorified in the Son; he rejects using the passage as a prosperity-style "ask for anything" verse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=13945" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:25</a> | Is it a sin for Christians to lend money with interest? | He says not inherently, though lending among friends and neighbors can still create practical and moral trouble. | He says lending with interest is not inherently sinful, but it can damage friendships and neighborly relationships. He explains that biblical usury laws often assume kind-for-kind lending or very slow inflation, whereas modern money loses value over time; that difference complicates applying ancient lending rules directly to modern cash loans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=14072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:32</a> | Did Jesus know about modern Western inflation? | No, not in modern economic terms; he says even later economists did not fully understand that, though ancient inflation existed. | He says no, not in the modern Western economic sense, and adds that economists did not really understand modern inflation until much later. However, he says inflation did exist in the ancient world, including Egypt and Rome; the difference is that ancient people experienced it without understanding its causes in modern terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=14120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:20</a> | Have you considered reviewing Richard Elliott Friedman's Exodus hypothesis? | He is not convinced by Friedman's view and thinks documentary-hypothesis commitments drive the reading too much. | He says he is not convinced by Friedman's Exodus hypothesis and thinks Friedman's documentary-hypothesis commitments drive the reading. He characterizes Friedman's lectures as heavy on documentary hypothesis and light on Exodus argumentation, and he is not especially interested in reviewing the view unless perhaps later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=14241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:21</a> | What are your thoughts on Rings of Power and Tolkien's philosophy? | He has not watched Rings of Power and describes Tolkien's outlook more as pastoral and anti-industrial than a formal philosophy. | He says he has not watched Rings of Power and has heard bad things about its second season. On Tolkien, he sees more of a pastoral, anti-industrial outlook than a tight formal philosophy, though he also notes Tolkien's religious ideology and concepts of sin as interesting and compelling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=14392" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:52</a> | How certain are you that Hammurabi was in Canaan in year 39? | He bases it on Hammurabi's year epithets, especially the year of subduing all enemies, while treating it as an inference. | He bases the inference on Hammurabi's year names or epithets, especially the year in which Hammurabi claims to have subdued all enemies. Falk says that wording implies a very broad campaign, making year 39 the best option among the available year epithets, though he still treats it as an inference rather than absolute proof. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pjs3dNgZMQA?t=14550" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:30</a> | Is Ishtar the same goddess as Isis? | No. He says they have different origins and mythologies, with only superficial similarity. | No. He identifies Ishtar as a Mesopotamian stellar deity and Isis as an Egyptian goddess tied to Osiris and the dead. Their origins, mythologies, and roles are different, and he says the only real similarity is the superficial fact that both are female deities. |
